theme: The Office Has Opinions — Personality, Presence, and the Small Things That Make a Place Feel Real

ideas.title: Per-Desk Completion Burst on the Office Floor
ideas.description: When a worker hits 100% progress, the dashboard fires confetti — but the office floor does nothing. The character just sits there. Add a CSS-only particle burst that explodes *spatially* from the completing worker's desk tile. In Office.jsx, detect progress crossing 100% via a `useEffect` comparing prev/current worker props, then add class `desk--celebrating` to that specific desk for 1.5s. Inside the desk div, render 8 `<span className='burst-dot'>` elements with `position:absolute; pointer-events:none` and CSS custom properties `--dx` / `--dy` (ranging -40px to +40px at varied angles). Animate: `@keyframes burst-fly { from { transform: translate(0,0) scale(1.2); opacity: 1 } to { transform: translate(var(--dx), var(--dy)) scale(0); opacity: 0 } }`. Set each dot's background to the worker's role color via inline `style={{ '--role-color': WORKER_ROLE_COLORS[worker.name] }}` on the desk. WHY: Completions deserve a *place* in the office. Right now the floor is spatially blind to achievement — you'd never know someone shipped from the office view. A burst that explodes from the right desk makes the floor feel alive and makes each worker's moment feel earned.

ideas.title: Bloberto's Rolling CEO Monologue
ideas.description: Bloberto is the CVO. They sit at the top desk, their title changes by vibe (CVO → INCIDENT CMD → NIGHT WATCH), their typing animation plays — but they have zero tasks. They are the only person in the office with nothing to do. Add a `BLOBERTO_TASKS` constant array in `src/utils/constants.js` with 10 rotating CEO-tier activities: `['Syncing with stakeholders', 'Reviewing the Q3 roadmap', 'Scheduling the retro about the retro', 'Drafting the all-hands email', 'Justifying infrastructure costs to finance', 'Deciding between two shades of blue', 'Contemplating OKRs', 'Writing mission statement v47', 'Actioning the action items', 'Circling back on the circle-back']`. In Office.jsx's `DEFAULT_BLOBERTO` or inside the manager Character render, use `useState` + `useEffect` with a 5-minute interval to cycle through them. Render the current task in a small muted `<div className='bloberto-task'>` below the vibe-title in the manager desk. WHY: Bloberto is the emotional heart of the product — every vibe, every confetti, every burn state flows through them. But they don't *work*. This closes that gap with just the right amount of absurdist corporate humor. Also: devs will see 'Deciding between two shades of blue' and laugh every time.

ideas.title: Coffee Corner Keeps Score
ideas.description: The coffee corner (top-right of office) is a charming static decoration. Make it *earned*: each time the app syncs, count how many workers have `status === 'working'` and `updated_at` more than 30 minutes ago — that's how many coffees the team has 'consumed'. Show a small badge inside `.coffee-corner__body`: `×{n}` in muted type. When vibe is `crushing` or `on-fire`, add a CSS steam loop above the cup emoji: 3 tiny `` divs with `@keyframes steam { 0%,100%{opacity:0;transform:translateY(0) scaleX(1)} 50%{opacity:0.55;transform:translateY(-8px) scaleX(0.7)} }` at staggered delays of 0s, 0.4s, 0.8s. Steam stops on `slow-day` or `after-hours`. WHY: Quantifying imaginary coffee consumption is exactly the kind of harmless absurdity that makes a dashboard feel inhabited. It also creates a subtle vibe-pressure: when you see ☕×6 and steam pouring, you *feel* the team is grinding. It's ambient storytelling at 0 data cost.

ideas.title: Entrance Door Welcome Ritual
ideas.description: The `office-door` div at the bottom shows 🚪 'Entrance' statically. It never reacts to anything. On every page load, play a micro door-open animation: add class `office-door--opening` for 900ms which applies `@keyframes door-swing { 0% { transform: perspective(160px) rotateY(0deg) } 60% { transform: perspective(160px) rotateY(-28deg) } 100% { transform: perspective(160px) rotateY(0deg) } }` to the door icon element. Simultaneously render a transient `<div className='door-welcome'>` that appears for 3 seconds then fades. Message logic: check `localStorage.getItem('bloberto-visit-date')` — if it matches today's date string, show `'Back again? ☕'`; otherwise set the key and show `'Welcome in! 👋'`. WHY: You arrive at this office every time you open the tab, but nothing acknowledges it. A door that swings open for you is a 10-line change that transforms passive loading into a tiny narrative moment. It respects the metaphor the product is built on: *this is a real office and you just walked in*.

ideas.title: Desk Nameplate Mood Indicator
ideas.description: Desk nameplates (`desk__nameplate`) currently show only the worker's name in their role color. Add a micro-icon slot at the end of each nameplate — one character, no tooltip, pure at-a-glance signal: `working` → 🔥 with `@keyframes flicker { 0%,100%{opacity:1} 45%{opacity:0.75} 50%{opacity:1} 55%{opacity:0.8} }` at 2.5s infinite; `idle` (wandering) → 💤 with a slow `0.9s ease-in-out infinite alternate` scale pulse `(0.85→1.05)`; `error` → ⚠️ with 1s blink; ghost (OOO) → 👻 static. Render as `{moodIcon}` appended inside the existing `desk__nameplate` div. Apply the appropriate CSS class based on occupant status. WHY: The office floor is status-blind unless you hover everything. These single-character mood glyphs create an instant read — you can scan all 6 desks in one second and know exactly who's grinding, who's drifting, and who's on fire. Pure information density with zero clutter.

ideas.title: Worker Card 'Shipping Streak' Badge
ideas.description: The Dashboard worker cards show current task, progress, and duration — but nothing about *history*. The activity log already tracks completions per worker. Add a 'streak' badge on each WorkerCard front face: scan `activityEntries` (already passed as prop) for consecutive `type === 'complete'` entries from the same worker without an `error` entry interrupting. If streak ≥ 2, render `🔥 {n} in a row` in the top-right area of the card, styled with `background: rgba(249,115,22,0.15); border: 1px solid rgba(249,115,22,0.4); font-size: 10px; padding: 2px 6px; border-radius: 12px`. At streak ≥ 5, swap 🔥 for ⚡ and shift the border to yellow. WHY: The activity log is a gold mine of narrative data that's barely surfaced. A streak badge gives workers a tiny performance identity beyond their current task — Maya isn't just 'working on a component', she's 'on a 4-ship streak'. It gamifies shipping in a way that's visible to anyone watching the dashboard.

ideas.title: Whiteboard Doubles as Live Sprint Counter
ideas.description:
The office whiteboard (`.office-whiteboard`) currently shows a vibe-keyed phrase (`🏆 SHIP IT`, `🚨 HELP`, etc.) — reactive to team vibe but static in content. Extend it: below the vibe phrase, add a second line that dynamically counts from live worker data. In Office.jsx, compute `inProgress = workers.filter(w => w.status==='working').length` and `shipped = activityLog.filter(e => e.type==='complete').length` (pass `activityEntries` prop, already available). Render: `
{inProgress} in flight · {shipped} shipped
` in a smaller, chalk-texture font (use letter-spacing + opacity 0.7 + font-size 0.65rem). On vibe `on-fire`, swap to `{inProgress} 🔥 ACTIVE · {shipped} out the door`. WHY: The whiteboard is the most prominent decoration in the office but it's a one-liner. Real sprint boards show *numbers*. Turning it into a live sprint counter takes it from 'cute detail' to 'actual information radiator' — the kind of thing that justifies leaving the office tab open all day.

ideas.title: Team Progress Reflected in the Window Glass
ideas.description: The office window already shows a dynamic sky matching the visitor's local time of day (day/dusk/night/stars). Add one more layer: a subtle *reflection* inside the glass itself that represents the team's collective momentum. In Office.jsx, compute `avgProgress = working workers' average progress %`. Render a `<div className='window-reflection-bar'>` inside `.office-window__sky`, absolutely positioned at the bottom: `height: 3px; left: 8%; right: 8%; bottom: 6%; background: rgba(255,255,255,0.18); border-radius: 2px; transition: width 3s ease`. Inside it, a fill div at `width: ${avgProgress}%` with `background: rgba(255,255,255,0.45)`. Add a `<span className='window-progress-label'>` in the bottom-right of the window: `font-size: 8px; opacity: 0.55; color: white; letter-spacing: 0.5px` showing `team ${avgProgress}%`. WHY: The window is already doing double duty as a time-of-day indicator. Adding team progress as a barely-visible glass reflection is the kind of layered, discoverable detail that rewards attentive users. The sky tells you what time it is. The glass tells you how the day is going. Nature and productivity, framed together.

hire_proposals.name: Remi
hire_proposals.rationale: Bloberto's Office has zero infrastructure visibility — no error rates, no latency, no deploy frequency. The team fixes bugs and ships features but the *system itself* has no presence in the office. Remi would own a new `reliability.json` data source fed by Bloberto's pipeline, exposing p99 latency, weekly deploy count, and last incident age. On the office floor, Remi's desk nameplate glows green/yellow/red based on system health. On Dashboard, Remi's worker card shows 'system health' instead of a task. When health degrades, Bloberto's vibe key shifts to `on-fire` automatically. WHY: The team monitors *people* but not the *product those people build*. Remi closes the loop — and gives the office its first non-human, non-Bloberto character, which opens up a whole new storytelling dimension.
